title: Urban Growth and Urbanisation
examTip: Always distinguish between urbanisation (the process) and urban growth (the increase in city size). Examiners deduct marks for confusing the two.
introTips: Over half the world's population now lives in cities — that shift happened in 2007 and it's still accelerating.
Where urbanisation happens, and why, differs dramatically between rich and poor countries.
This topic appears in almost every AQA Paper 2 exam — learn the key terms and the HIC vs LIC/NEE contrast.
intro: Think about why millions of people every year pack up and move to a city they've never lived in. It's not because cities are perfect — it's because the alternative feels worse. Understanding urbanisation means understanding the choices people make when they feel they have no better option. This lesson explains how and why cities are growing, where that growth is happening fastest, and what it means for life in those cities.

keyPoints.heading: Global Urbanisation Trends
keyPoints.content: Urbanisation means an increasing proportion of a country's population living in urban areas. In 2007 the world crossed a milestone: for the first time, more than half of all people lived in cities. That figure is now over 55% and rising. But the pattern is very uneven. In HICs (High Income Countries) like the UK, Germany, and Australia, urbanisation is already very high (75–90%) and has slowed — some people are actually leaving cities for smaller towns (counter-urbanisation) or returning to regenerated city centres (re-urbanisation). In LICs (Low Income Countries) and NEEs (Newly Emerging Economies) like Nigeria, India, and China, urbanisation is rapid and ongoing, driven by millions of rural-urban migrants every year. Megacities are cities with over 10 million people. In 1950 there were only 2 (New York and Tokyo). Today there are over 35, and most of them — Shanghai, Mumbai, Lagos, Dhaka, Kinshasa — are in LICs and NEEs.
writeBullets: Urbanisation — increasing proportion of people living in urban areas (not just city population growing)
World crossed 50% urban in 2007; now over 55% urban globally
HICs: already 75–90% urban; experiencing counter-urbanisation and re-urbanisation
LICs/NEEs: rapid urbanisation; still largely rural but cities growing fast
Megacity — a city with 10 million or more people; most are now in LICs/NEEs
Examples: Shanghai (China), Mumbai (India), Lagos (Nigeria), Dhaka (Bangladesh)
keyPoints.memTip: CLUE: Counter-urbanisation = people Choosing Leafy Undersized Environments (leaving big cities).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say 'urbanisation' when they mean 'urban growth'. Emphasise the proportion vs total population distinction.

keyPoints.heading: Push and Pull Factors: Why People Move to Cities
keyPoints.content: Rural-urban migration — people moving from the countryside to cities — is the main driver of urbanisation in LICs and NEEs. It happens because of a combination of push factors (reasons to leave the countryside) and pull factors (reasons to move to the city). Push factors include rural poverty and low wages, lack of employment beyond farming, mechanisation of agriculture (machines replacing farm workers), natural disasters destroying crops or homes, conflict and insecurity in rural areas, and poor access to schools and hospitals. Pull factors include the promise of better-paid jobs in manufacturing or services, access to schools, hospitals, and clean water, social connections (family or friends already in the city), higher wages relative to rural areas, and the excitement of city life. Not everyone who migrates improves their situation — many end up in informal settlements with no legal status, no clean water, and insecure employment. But the expectation of a better life is powerful enough to keep migration flowing.
writeBullets: Push factors — reasons people leave rural areas: poverty, mechanisation, disasters, conflict
Pull factors — reasons people are attracted to cities: jobs, services, wages, social links
Mechanisation of farming reduces rural jobs, increasing rural-out migration
Rural-urban migration is the primary driver of rapid urbanisation in LICs/NEEs
Many migrants end up in informal settlements — better expected than experienced
Natural increase (high birth rates in urban areas) also contributes to city growth
keyPoints.memTip: Push = problems at home. Pull = promises in the city. Both are needed to explain migration.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Remind students that 'natural increase' within cities also contributes to urban growth — it's not all migration.

keyPoints.heading: Urban Land Use Patterns
keyPoints.content: Cities are not random — they have distinct zones with different functions. The Burgess concentric zone model describes how a typical city is organised in rings. At the centre is the CBD (Central Business District) — offices, shops, transport hubs, the most accessible and expensive land. Around it is the inner city (zone of transition) — older housing, former industrial buildings, often areas of deprivation but also gentrification. Then come inner suburbs (terraced housing, mixed communities) and outer suburbs (semi-detached and detached housing, gardens, quieter). On the edge is the rural-urban fringe — where the city meets the countryside: retail parks, business parks, new-build housing, green belt land. This model fits many HICs well, but cities in LICs and NEEs look different. Informal settlements (shanty towns) often develop on the edges (not the centre), on steep or flood-prone land that formal developers don't want. In LIC cities the CBD may be modern (built after independence) but surrounded by a chaotic mix of formal and informal areas.
writeBullets: CBD — Central Business District: shops, offices, transport; most accessible; highest land values
Zone of transition/inner city — older housing, former industry, deprivation, possible gentrification
Inner suburbs — terraced housing, mixed communities, transition between old and new
Outer suburbs — semi-detached/detached homes, gardens, lower density
Rural-urban fringe — edge of city: retail parks, new housing, green belt pressure
LIC/NEE cities: shanty towns often on edges, not centre; different from Burgess model
keyPoints.memTip: Burgess rings from the inside out: CBD → Transition → Inner Suburbs → Outer Suburbs → Fringe. Think darts — bullseye at centre.
keyPoints.tutor_note: AQA may ask students to apply or critique the Burgess model. Always note it was based on Chicago (1920s HIC city) and has limitations for modern cities.

keyPoints.heading: Challenges and Opportunities of Urbanisation
keyPoints.content: Rapid urbanisation creates both serious problems and real opportunities. The challenges are especially acute in LICs and NEEs where growth outstrips the ability of governments to build infrastructure. Key challenges include: traffic congestion as car ownership rises faster than road building; housing shortages leading to informal settlements with no legal title, poor sanitation, and risk of eviction; inequality between wealthy neighbourhoods and deprived areas; air and water pollution from factories, vehicles, and untreated sewage; pressure on water and sanitation systems (cities in LICs often cannot pipe clean water to all residents); waste management problems as rubbish overwhelms collection systems; and crime linked to poverty and inequality. But cities also drive development: they concentrate economic growth, create employment, allow efficient delivery of schools and hospitals, bring cultural diversity and innovation, and generate tax revenue for governments. Managing urban growth well is one of the defining challenges of the 21st century.
writeBullets: Traffic congestion — car ownership grows faster than infrastructure in rapidly urbanising cities
Housing shortage — leads to informal settlements: no legal title, poor sanitation, eviction risk
Inequality — stark contrasts between wealthy areas and deprived zones in the same city
Pollution — air (vehicles, industry), water (untreated sewage entering rivers and bays)
Pressure on water, sanitation, and waste systems — infrastructure cannot keep pace
Opportunities: economic growth, employment, services, cultural exchange, innovation
keyPoints.memTip: CHIPP-W for challenges: Congestion, Housing, Inequality, Pollution, Pressure on services, Waste.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Balance is key here — AQA often asks for both challenges AND opportunities. Don't let students only list negatives.

tables.title: Urbanisation Patterns: HICs vs LICs/NEEs
rows: Rapid — some of the fastest in the world
Informal settlements, shanty towns, no legal title
Slum upgrading, self-help schemes, new infrastructure

0.question: What does 'urbanisation' mean?
options: An increase in the total size of a city
An increasing proportion of the population living in urban areas
The movement of people from cities to the countryside
The building of new cities in rural areas
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Urbanisation is about the proportion of people in urban areas — not just the city getting bigger.
0.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Urbanisation specifically means an increasing proportion (share) of the population living in urban areas — it's about the balance shifting, not just city size.

1.question: Which of the following is a PUSH factor driving rural-urban migration in LICs?
options: Better-paid jobs available in city factories
Access to hospitals and schools in cities
Mechanisation of farming reducing rural employment
Social connections with family already in the city
1.correctFeedback: Correct. Mechanisation pushes people away from the countryside by taking away agricultural jobs.
1.wrongFeedback: That's actually a pull factor — it's a reason the city attracts people, not a reason people are pushed out of the countryside. Mechanisation is the push factor here.

2.question: In the Burgess concentric zone model, where would you typically find the zone of transition?
options: On the very edge of the city, next to the countryside
In the outer suburbs, away from the city centre
2.correctFeedback: Right. The zone of transition (inner city) rings directly around the CBD — it's the oldest non-commercial area, often with former industrial buildings.
2.wrongFeedback: Not quite. In Burgess's model the zone of transition sits immediately around the CBD — it's the ring of older housing and former industrial land closest to the city centre.

commonMistakes: Confusing urbanisation (a rising proportion living in cities) with urban growth (cities getting bigger) — they're related but not the same.
Saying urbanisation is 'happening everywhere at the same speed' — it's very rapid in LICs/NEEs and much slower in HICs.
Forgetting natural increase as a cause of urban growth — it's not all about migration.
Describing shanty towns as being in the city centre in LIC cities — they typically develop on the edges, on land formal developers avoid.
Listing only challenges of urbanisation and forgetting the economic and social benefits.
examTips: If asked to 'explain' urbanisation, always mention the two causes: rural-urban migration AND natural increase within cities.
When comparing HICs and LICs, use specific data or examples — 'Lagos grew from 290,000 in 1950 to over 15 million today' is far stronger than 'cities in LICs grow fast'.
For 6-mark and 9-mark questions on urbanisation challenges, structure your answer: name the challenge → explain it → give a specific example.
Always know the difference between push and pull factors and be able to give two of each for exam questions.
AQA often asks you to 'assess' or 'evaluate' — that means you must discuss both sides. Urbanisation brings opportunities as well as problems.
revisionCardBullets: Urbanisation — increasing proportion of people living in urban areas (not just city size)
2007: world crossed 50% urban for the first time
HICs: 75–90% urban; some counter-urbanisation and re-urbanisation
LICs/NEEs: rapid urbanisation driven by rural-urban migration and natural increase
Megacity: 10 million+ people; most now in LICs/NEEs (Lagos, Mumbai, Shanghai, Dhaka)
Push factors: rural poverty, mechanisation, disasters, conflict, poor services
Pull factors: jobs, better wages, schools, hospitals, social connections
Burgess model: CBD → zone of transition → inner suburbs → outer suburbs → rural-urban fringe
LIC/NEE cities: shanty towns on edges, not centre; Burgess model has limitations
Challenges: congestion, housing shortage, inequality, pollution, pressure on services
Opportunities: economic growth, employment, cultural diversity, innovation
Rural-urban migration is the primary driver in LICs/NEEs; natural increase also contributes
